window: curated via the openai-complaints skill (GitHub issues from the last ~2 months plus older notable Hacker News / status-page items)
note: Curated by the openai-complaints skill from PUBLIC sources: genuine bug reports and feature requests filed by real developers in OpenAI's official Azure-style GitHub SDK repo (openai/openai-python — many labelled 'bug'), plus public Hacker News threads and OpenAI's own status page. Every item links to its original public post and is presented as user-reported. Most SDK items are single-source (verified=false); the API-outage item is corroborated by OpenAI's official status page and the Codex-backend crash is corroborated by several independent reports (verified=true). NOTE: a large share of these come from the openai-python tracker, so they skew toward SDK / client-library issues rather than core-model quality; the Hacker News items cover the API surface (rate limits, outages, pricing/lock-in).

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that non-streaming OpenAI API calls (Responses API with medium/high reasoning) silently hang forever when run behind a NAT gateway: the server finishes generating (visible in the dashboard) but the client never receives the response because the default httpx transport has no TCP keepalive, so NAT drops the idle connection during the 300-700s generation.
complaints.quote: Non-streaming OpenAI API calls ... hang indefinitely when run behind a NAT gateway. The OpenAI server successfully generates the response — visible in the dashboard — but the client never receives it and blocks forever.

complaints.complaint: Multiple developers independently reported that client.responses.stream() crashes with 'TypeError: NoneType object is not iterable' when the terminal response.completed event carries output=null (seen against the ChatGPT Codex backend), because parse_response iterates response.output without a null guard — killing the whole stream even though the items were already delivered.
complaints.quote: client.responses.stream() raises TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable when the server emits a response.completed event in which response.output is None.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported a breaking change: passing an Azure AD bearer token via api_key to AzureOpenAI worked in openai==2.33.0 but returns 401 Unauthorized in 2.34.0 and later, because the client now consistently sends api_key as the 'api-key' header instead of 'Authorization: Bearer', with no clear changelog warning.
complaints.quote: After upgrading from openai==2.33.0 to openai==2.34.0, requests made with AzureOpenAI started returning 401 Unauthorized. The same code works with 2.33.0 and fails with 2.34.0.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that the Python SDK force-closes the streaming connection (sends a TCP FIN) immediately after the '[DONE]' SSE event without draining the HTTP chunked terminator, producing 'RemoteProtocolError: peer closed connection without sending complete message body' on clients and a spike of 'downstream_remote_disconnect' on upstream proxies — a regression from a 2025 change.
complaints.quote: The client emits TCP FIN immediately after receiving data: [DONE], while the server-side chunked terminator is still being flushed ... the proxy records downstream_remote_disconnect.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that client.images.edit() rejects an image_url input with a 400 error even though the official API reference documents image_url as a valid (JSON) input for /images/edits, concluding the documentation is wrong.
complaints.quote: client.images.edit() method failed to pass image_url as image input ... I am afraid /images/edits doesn't support image_url as image input. Doc is wrong.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that the 'Nested params' example in the official README is not runnable: it calls client.chat.responses.create(), which does not exist (raises AttributeError), and mixes Responses API and Chat Completions parameters in a single snippet.
complaints.quote: The 'Nested params' example in README.md is not runnable as written. It calls client.chat.responses.create(...), but the Chat resource only exposes completions ... raises AttributeError: 'Chat' object has no attribute 'responses'.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that requesting reasoning summaries (summary="auto") on the Responses API never emits any reasoning-summary events on the gpt-5.3-codex / gpt-5.1-codex models (the stream goes straight to output text), while the exact same code reliably emits summary events on gpt-5-mini.
complaints.quote: The codex variants never emit response.reasoning_summary_part.added / response.reasoning_summary_text.delta ... The exact same calling code on gpt-5-mini emits reasoning summary events reliably.

complaints.complaint: A developer (writing on behalf of Baseten) reported that the OpenAI API has no proactive cancellation signal: when a client times out, the server keeps doing expensive inference that nobody will receive, and requested a 'Request-Timeout-Ms' header so gateways can cancel in-flight work instead of only reacting to a TCP disconnect.
complaints.quote: When a client times out, the server has no idea ... It keeps doing expensive work (inference, token generation) that nobody will ever receive. The only signal today is a TCP disconnect — reactive, not proactive.

complaints.complaint: Developers on Hacker News reported the OpenAI API being down and noted poor uptime, pointing at OpenAI's own status page incident ('Increased errors across API and ChatGPT') and joking that the API should be expected to go down regularly.
complaints.quote: The OpenAI API is currently down. Also, the uptime graph for November doesn't look so great.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that OpenAI's Whisper API rate limit of 50 requests per minute effectively caps a consumer app at ~50 concurrent users, making it a non-starter for building consumer-scale products, and noted GPT-4 had similarly tight limits.
complaints.quote: OpenAI whisper api has 50 request per min rate limit. It means that I can't have more than 50 concurrent users. If you are building a consumer app, this seems like a no go.

complaints.complaint: A developer reported that the OpenAI API is heavily rate limited because of overwhelming demand, to the point of being problematic for users trying to build on it.
complaints.quote: My own observation while using the OpenAI API is that it is massively rate limited due to the enormous demand ... its problematic for users.

complaints.complaint: A developer cautioned that building on the OpenAI API carries vendor lock-in risk: OpenAI can raise prices, ban a customer, or change the model overnight (degrading prompt results), and there is no drop-in alternative that returns the same results.
complaints.quote: What happens if OpenAI decides to increase the price on you? ... What happens if OpenAI changes their model over night and now you are getting bad responses ... there is no alternatives to OpenAI's API.

complaints.complaint: A developer described OpenAI's usage-tier system as annoying, recalling waiting periods and spend thresholds to unlock higher rate limits — to the point of deliberately spending credits on junk API calls just to reach the next tier.
complaints.quote: I remember at one point spamming the OpenAI API with garbage just to consume credits in order to get to the next tier to increase rate limits.
